title: A Mentorship Session
summary: A senior mentor guides a junior colleague through career challenges and professional development strategies.

body.speaker: Mentor
body.text: Come in, Sarah. I've been looking forward to our session today.

body.speaker: Mentee
body.text: Thanks for making time, David. I've been struggling with a few things and wasn't sure who else to turn to.

body.speaker: Mentor
body.text: That's exactly what I'm here for. What's been weighing on you?

body.speaker: Mentee
body.text: Honestly, it's the leadership project. I feel like the team doesn't take me seriously, and if I hadn't pushed so hard last quarter, the whole initiative would have collapsed.

body.speaker: Mentor
body.text: I've noticed that pattern as well. Tell me — when you say they don't take you seriously, what specific situations come to mind?

body.speaker: Mentee
body.text: Well, in meetings, my suggestions are often overlooked until someone more senior repeats the same idea. It's deeply frustrating.

body.speaker: Mentor
body.text: That experience is more common than you might think, and it has been reported by many emerging leaders at your stage. However, the way you respond to it will define how quickly you grow.

body.speaker: Mentee
body.text: What do you mean by that? I already try to speak up more confidently.

body.speaker: Mentor
body.text: Confidence matters, but so does framing. If you had presented your ideas with explicit data and connected them to the company's strategic goals, they would have been harder to dismiss.

body.speaker: Mentee
body.text: I hadn't thought about it that way. I assumed good ideas would speak for themselves.

body.speaker: Mentor
body.text: That's a common assumption, but influence is built through credibility and context, not just content. You need to be seen as someone who understands the bigger picture.

body.speaker: Mentee
body.text: So you're saying I should spend more time preparing the business case before raising ideas in the room?

body.speaker: Mentor
body.text: Precisely. And it also helps to build alliances beforehand. If you brief two or three key stakeholders before a meeting, your proposals are far less likely to be dismissed.

body.speaker: Mentee
body.text: That makes a lot of sense. I've been told that politics in the workplace is something to avoid, but maybe I've misunderstood what that really means.

body.speaker: Mentor
body.text: There's a difference between manipulation and strategic relationship-building. The latter is not only acceptable — it's essential.

body.speaker: Mentee
body.text: What about the feeling of imposter syndrome? I sometimes wonder whether I actually deserve to be in the rooms I'm invited into.

body.speaker: Mentor
body.text: Nearly every high performer I've ever mentored has said the same thing. The fact that you question yourself actually signals self-awareness, which is a strength, not a weakness.

body.speaker: Mentee
body.text: I hadn't considered it from that angle. So what would you recommend as my next concrete step?

body.speaker: Mentor
body.text: I'd suggest you identify one project where you can demonstrate measurable impact over the next sixty days. Document your process and outcomes so that your contribution is visible and undeniable.

body.speaker: Mentee
body.text: Sixty days feels manageable. I'll start drafting a plan this week.

body.speaker: Mentor
body.text: Good. And remember — if you hadn't sought this conversation, none of this clarity would have emerged. Taking initiative is already a sign you're on the right path.

questions.prompt: What is Sarah's main concern at the start of the session?
options: Her team does not take her seriously
She disagrees with David's management style
She is thinking of leaving the company
questions.explanation: Sarah says she feels the team doesn't take her seriously and that the initiative would have collapsed without her effort.
questions.hint: Listen to Sarah's opening complaint about meetings.

questions.prompt: According to Sarah, what happens to her suggestions in meetings?
options: They are praised but not acted upon
They are overlooked until repeated by a more senior person
questions.explanation: Sarah says her suggestions are often overlooked until someone more senior repeats the same idea.
questions.hint: Think about who eventually gets credit for her ideas.

questions.prompt: What does David say has been reported by many emerging leaders?
questions.explanation: David says Sarah's experience is more common than she might think and has been reported by many emerging leaders.
questions.hint: David uses the passive voice to describe what others have experienced.

questions.prompt: What conditional does David use to explain how Sarah's ideas could have been more persuasive?
options: If you present data, your ideas will be accepted
If you had presented data, your ideas would have been harder to dismiss
If you present data, your ideas would be accepted
If you had presented data, your ideas will be stronger
questions.explanation: David uses a third conditional: 'If you had presented your ideas with explicit data... they would have been harder to dismiss.'
questions.hint: This is a third conditional referring to a past situation.

questions.prompt: What does David say influence is built through?
options: Credibility and context, not just content
questions.explanation: David explicitly states that influence is built through credibility and context, not just content.
questions.hint: David corrects Sarah's assumption about good ideas.

questions.prompt: What strategy does David recommend before raising ideas in a meeting?
questions.explanation: David advises Sarah to brief two or three key stakeholders before a meeting so her proposals are less likely to be dismissed.
questions.hint: David talks about building alliances before the meeting.

questions.prompt: What distinction does David draw regarding workplace politics?
options: Politics is only acceptable at senior levels
questions.explanation: David draws a clear distinction between manipulation and strategic relationship-building, calling the latter essential.
questions.hint: David corrects Sarah's view of workplace politics.

questions.prompt: How does David reframe Sarah's experience of imposter syndrome?
options: As a sign she is unprepared
As evidence of self-awareness, which is a strength
As a common but serious problem
As something that will pass with time
questions.explanation: David says that questioning oneself signals self-awareness, which is a strength, not a weakness.
questions.hint: David offers a positive reinterpretation of doubt.

questions.prompt: What concrete action does David suggest Sarah take?
options: Request a formal review from HR
Identify a project where she can demonstrate measurable impact in sixty days
questions.explanation: David specifically recommends identifying one project where she can demonstrate measurable impact over sixty days.
questions.hint: David gives a specific time frame.

questions.prompt: What does David say Sarah should document?
options: Her process and outcomes so her contribution is visible
The feedback she receives from colleagues
questions.explanation: David tells Sarah to document her process and outcomes so that her contribution is visible and undeniable.
questions.hint: David wants her contribution to be impossible to ignore.

questions.prompt: What does Sarah say she will do this week?
options: Request a meeting with her manager
Sign up for a training course
questions.explanation: Sarah says she will start drafting a plan this week after David's sixty-day challenge.
questions.hint: Sarah responds positively and commits to a first step.

questions.prompt: What third conditional does David use at the very end of the conversation?
options: If you hadn't asked, you wouldn't have learned
If you hadn't sought this conversation, none of this clarity would have emerged
If you had come earlier, things would be better
If you hadn't worked hard, you would have failed
questions.explanation: David closes with: 'If you hadn't sought this conversation, none of this clarity would have emerged.' — a classic third conditional.
questions.hint: Listen to David's final encouraging remark.

questions.prompt: What assumption did Sarah previously hold about good ideas?
options: They need strong data to be accepted
They must be endorsed by management
questions.explanation: Sarah admits she assumed good ideas would speak for themselves, which David challenges.
questions.hint: Sarah acknowledges a naive assumption she had held.

questions.prompt: What is the overall purpose of this mentorship session?
options: To help Sarah build her leadership presence and influence
To discuss a specific project failure
questions.explanation: The session focuses on helping Sarah become a more credible and influential emerging leader.
questions.hint: Consider the broad themes of the entire conversation.

questions.prompt: Which phrase best describes David's mentoring style in this session?
questions.explanation: David listens carefully, normalises Sarah's feelings, and offers practical, strategic advice — a blend of empathy and guidance.
questions.hint: Think about both David's tone and the type of advice he offers.

cloze.template: During the mentorship session, Sarah admitted that she had been {{b1}} with her leadership project and that the team didn't seem to take her {{b2}}. David pointed out that this experience had been {{b3}} by many emerging leaders. He explained that if Sarah had {{b4}} her ideas with explicit data, they would have been harder to {{b5}}. David stressed that influence is built through {{b6}} and context, not just content. He recommended that Sarah {{b7}} key stakeholders before important meetings, so that her proposals would be far less likely to be {{b8}}. When Sarah raised the topic of {{b9}} syndrome, David reframed it as a sign of {{b10}}, calling it a strength rather than a weakness. He then suggested she identify one project where she could {{b11}} measurable impact over the next sixty {{b12}} and document her {{b13}} and outcomes so that her contribution remained {{b14}}. Finally, David reminded Sarah that if she hadn't {{b15}} this conversation, none of the clarity they had reached would have {{b16}}.

blanks.explanation: Sarah says she has been 'struggling' with the leadership project.
Sarah says the team doesn't take her 'seriously'.
David uses the passive: 'it has been reported by many emerging leaders'.
David says: 'If you had presented your ideas with explicit data...' — third conditional.
David says the ideas would have been harder to 'dismiss'.
David says 'influence is built through credibility and context'.
David recommends Sarah 'brief' two or three key stakeholders before meetings.
David says proposals would be 'far less likely to be dismissed'.
Sarah raises the topic of 'imposter syndrome'.
David says Sarah should 'demonstrate' measurable impact.
David says 'document your process and outcomes'.
David says her contribution should be 'visible and undeniable'.
David uses: 'if you hadn't sought this conversation' — third conditional.
David says 'none of this clarity would have emerged'.

annotations.meaningVi: đang khiến bạn lo lắng / đè nặng
annotations.exampleEn: Is something weighing on you? You seem distracted today.
It took years before her colleagues began to take her seriously.
The company invests heavily in developing emerging leaders.

annotations.meaningVi: tự chứng minh giá trị của mình
annotations.exampleEn: His results speak for themselves — no further explanation is needed.

annotations.meaningVi: cách trình bày / định khung vấn đề
annotations.exampleEn: The framing of her proposal made the risks seem manageable.

annotations.meaningVi: luận điểm kinh doanh / lý do thuyết phục về mặt kinh doanh
annotations.exampleEn: She built a strong business case for hiring an additional engineer.

annotations.meaningVi: xây dựng liên minh / tạo quan hệ đồng thuận
annotations.exampleEn: Successful managers know how to build alliances across departments.
We need to consult all stakeholders before making this decision.

annotations.meaningVi: xây dựng mối quan hệ có chiến lược
annotations.exampleEn: Strategic relationship-building helped her advance rapidly in the organisation.

annotations.meaningVi: hội chứng kẻ giả mạo (cảm giác không xứng đáng)
annotations.exampleEn: Imposter syndrome is common among first-generation professionals.
Self-awareness is the foundation of effective leadership.

annotations.meaningVi: tác động có thể đo lường được
annotations.exampleEn: The programme had a measurable impact on student performance.

annotations.meaningVi: rõ ràng và không thể phủ nhận
annotations.exampleEn: Her contribution was visible and undeniable to the entire team.
Taking initiative early in your career shows strong leadership potential.

annotations.meaningVi: bỏ qua / không chú ý đến
annotations.exampleEn: Her contribution was overlooked in the final report.

annotations.meaningVi: sự đáng tin cậy / uy tín
annotations.exampleEn: Years of consistent performance built her credibility with senior management.

annotations.meaningVi: người hoàn thành xuất sắc / nhân viên hiệu suất cao
annotations.exampleEn: High performers often question whether they truly deserve their success.
Your progress so far shows you're on the right path.

grammarNotes.title: Third Conditional (Unreal Past)
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 3 diễn tả tình huống không có thật trong quá khứ và kết quả giả định. Cấu trúc: If + had + V3, would have + V3. Ví dụ trong bài: 'If you had presented your ideas with explicit data, they would have been harder to dismiss.' Đây cũng kết hợp với thể bị động (would have been dismissed).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The third conditional describes hypothetical past situations and their imagined results. Structure: If + past perfect, would have + past participle. Example from the lesson: 'If you hadn't sought this conversation, none of this clarity would have emerged.' It is often combined with the passive voice for a more formal tone.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice in Reporting
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thể bị động thường được dùng để nói về trải nghiệm chung mà không cần nêu chủ thể cụ thể. Cấu trúc: has/have been + V3. Ví dụ: 'It has been reported by many emerging leaders.' Thể bị động tạo sự khách quan và nhấn mạnh vào kết quả thay vì người thực hiện.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passive voice is commonly used in professional contexts to describe general experiences without specifying a doer. Structure: has/have been + past participle. Example: 'That experience has been reported by many emerging leaders.' The passive shifts focus from the agent to the action or result, lending a more formal, objective tone.

translationVi: Trong buổi cố vấn, Sarah thú nhận rằng cô đang gặp khó khăn với dự án lãnh đạo và cảm thấy như đội nhóm không coi trọng cô. David chỉ ra rằng trải nghiệm này rất phổ biến và đã được nhiều nhà lãnh đạo trẻ đề cập. Ông giải thích rằng nếu Sarah đã trình bày ý tưởng với dữ liệu cụ thể, chúng sẽ khó bị bác bỏ hơn. David nhấn mạnh rằng tầm ảnh hưởng được xây dựng qua uy tín và ngữ cảnh, không chỉ nội dung. Ông khuyên Sarah nên thông báo trước cho các bên liên quan chính trước các cuộc họp quan trọng. Khi Sarah đề cập đến hội chứng kẻ giả mạo, David định khung lại đó là dấu hiệu của sự tự nhận thức — một điểm mạnh. Cuối cùng, ông gợi ý cô xác định một dự án để thể hiện tác động có thể đo lường trong vòng sáu mươi ngày, ghi lại quá trình và kết quả để đóng góp của mình trở nên rõ ràng và không thể phủ nhận.
criticalThinkingQuestion: David suggests that 'strategic relationship-building' is essential for professional advancement, while Sarah initially viewed workplace politics negatively. To what extent do you think navigating organizational politics is a necessary leadership skill, and where should the ethical boundaries lie?
